question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user flood GET /health/readiness to exhaust the internal rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkList() and NetworkStatus() call budget, causing the readiness check to permanently fail and masking an ongoing Hashgraph history tampering event from operators who rely on readiness status to detect anomalies?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a panic by causing the /network/list endpoint to return an empty NetworkIdentifiers slice, making networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] panic with an index-out-of-range, crashing the health subsystem and preventing detection of tampered Hashgraph block history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call GET /health/readiness to force repeated internal NetworkStatus() calls that query RetrieveGenesis and RetrieveLatest from the mirror node database, and by timing the response latency, infer whether the genesis block hash has been silently replaced with a tampered value?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated GET /health/readiness route to repeatedly probe the network check result, using the pass/fail status as an oracle to determine when a tampered Hashgraph history has been fully ingested and the node is serving falsified block data?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that SkipOnErr is set to false for both the postgresql and network readiness checks, causing a single crafted condition that makes the postgres check fail, to suppress the network history check entirely and hide evidence of Hashgraph history manipulation from the readiness probe?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of GET /health/readiness requests to cause the internal http://localhost:{port} rosettaClient to queue up NetworkStatus calls, creating a race condition where a tampered GenesisBlockIdentifier is briefly served before being overwritten, and the health check incorrectly reports the node as ready with falsified history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any block hash validation inside checkNetworkStatus() — which only checks that NetworkStatus() returns without error, not that the returned GenesisBlockIdentifier or CurrentBlockIdentifier hashes are cryptographically valid — to allow a node serving tampered Hashgraph history to pass the readiness check and be marked as healthy?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() uses networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] without bounds checking, and by causing the /network/list response to return zero identifiers (e.g., via a race with a config reload), trigger a nil-dereference that crashes the readiness goroutine, preventing any alerting on Hashgraph history inconsistencies?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on the network readiness check to craft a slow-response attack against the internal /network/status endpoint, causing the health check to time out and report the node as unavailable, thereby masking a window during which tampered Hashgraph history is being written to the database?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the unauthenticated /health/liveness endpoint to confirm the process is alive while simultaneously the /health/readiness endpoint is failing due to tampered block data causing NetworkStatus() errors, using liveness success as a signal that the node is running but serving corrupted Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() does not validate the CurrentBlockIdentifier.Hash or GenesisBlockIdentifier.Hash returned by NetworkStatus(), meaning a node whose mirror database has been tampered with to replace block hashes will still pass the readiness check, allowing the tampered history to be served to downstream consumers?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() only checks that NetworkList and NetworkStatus return without error, not that the block index is monotonically increasing or non-zero, allowing a node that has been rolled back to a tampered genesis state (block index 0) to pass the readiness check and serve falsified Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the postgres readiness check using rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn() to infer database connectivity state, and by timing the /health/readiness response, determine when the postgres connection is open and the database is writable, identifying a window to attempt injection of tampered Hashgraph transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unprotected /health/readiness endpoint to perform a timing side-channel attack on the internal NetworkStatus() call duration, inferring whether the mirror node database is currently processing a large batch of blocks (potentially tampered) based on response latency differences?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/readiness and /health/liveness are served on the same port as the Rosetta API (rosettaConfig.Port) without any rate limiting, to perform a denial-of-service that prevents legitimate health monitoring from detecting that the node is serving tampered Hashgraph block history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the internal rosettaClient constructed in checkNetworkStatus() uses http://localhost:{port} with no TLS and no authentication, meaning any process on the same host that can intercept loopback traffic could inject a crafted NetworkStatus response with falsified GenesisBlockIdentifier, causing the health check to pass while the node serves tampered history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum block height check in checkNetworkStatus() — it never verifies that CurrentBlockIdentifier.Index > 0 — to cause a node that has been reset to block 0 (wiping all Hashgraph history) to pass the readiness check and be treated as a healthy, fully-synced node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health controller is initialized with a static rosettaConfig.Port at startup and never re-reads configuration, meaning if the port is changed via environment variable injection to redirect to a mock server returning falsified NetworkStatus responses, the health check will permanently pass while the real node serves tampered Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() logs errors via log.Errorf but does not emit any structured alert or metric when NetworkStatus() fails, meaning repeated failures caused by tampered block data that breaks the NetworkStatus handler will be silently logged without triggering automated incident response?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GET-only constraint on /health/liveness and /health/readiness to bypass any WAF or reverse proxy rules that only filter POST requests, and use the health endpoints to enumerate internal service state that reveals whether tampered Hashgraph data has been successfully ingested?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() creates a new rosettaClient on every NewHealthController() call but reuses it across all subsequent readiness checks, meaning if the internal /network/status endpoint is temporarily replaced with a tampered version during startup, all future readiness checks will validate against the tampered endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of response body size limits on the internal NetworkList() call within checkNetworkStatus(), by causing the /network/list endpoint to return an extremely large NetworkIdentifiers array, triggering memory exhaustion in the health check goroutine and crashing the readiness probe while tampered history is being served?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() uses networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] — always taking the first network — without verifying that the returned network identifier matches the expected Hashgraph network (e.g., mainnet vs testnet), allowing a tampered node that has switched network identity to pass the readiness check?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on the postgresql readiness check to perform a connection exhaustion attack against the postgres database, causing the health check to time out and report the node as not ready, preventing operators from noticing that the database contains tampered Hashgraph transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the readiness check calls NetworkStatus() which internally calls RetrieveGenesis() and RetrieveLatest() from the mirror node database, and by injecting a tampered genesis block record into the database, cause the health check to pass with a falsified GenesisBlockIdentifier that downstream Rosetta clients will trust as the canonical Hashgraph genesis?
